message.content:
You are assessing one Coachella Valley dining establishment for an honest category-map artifact. The artifact's job is to map who's there, who wins what, and where the visibility gaps are.

Entity: Cuistot (Palm Desert)
Cuisine/type signal: French fine dining
Why it surfaced in discovery: Longtime French restaurant opened by Chef-Owner Bernard Dervieux in 1987 (apprenticed under Paul Bocuse); moved in 2003 to its current rustic French farmhouse building at El Paseo and Highway 111. Among the desert's most acclaimed restaurants with a 650+ wine list.
Discovery sources: https://greatchefs.com/chef/bernard-dervieux-food-recipes/ | https://www.cuistotrestaurant.bar/ | https://vacationpalmsprings.com/restaurant/cuistot-restaurant

Verify the entity exists, then assess on four dimensions. Use WebSearch (ToolSearch query "WebSearch") and WebFetch (ToolSearch query "WebFetch") to investigate. Read the entity's own website. Read 2-3 review-platform pages. Run regional queries to test visibility.

REPUTATION:
- review_volume — approximate Google/Yelp review count band (e.g., "3000+", "500-1000", "<100"). Mark [UNVERIFIED] if you can't find it.
- sentiment — current sentiment summary (positive / mostly positive / mixed / declining / negative). Recent reviews weighted over stale.
- recency_signal — what do reviews in the last 6-12 months say?
- longevity — years operating, ownership stability. Mark [UNVERIFIED] if unclear.
- credentials — earned third-party recognition (James Beard nominee/winner, Michelin, Food Network, Yelp Top 100, OpenTable Top, Desert Sun / Palm Springs Life awards, local press features). Empty array if none. Do NOT invent credentials.

AGENT_VISIBILITY:
- appears_in_queries — which regional queries actually surface this entity? Test specific queries: "best restaurants Coachella Valley", "fine dining Palm Desert", "[entity's cuisine] near Palm Springs", "date night Rancho Mirage", "best breakfast [city]".
- description_accuracy — when it surfaces, is the description correct?
- site_structured_data — fetch the entity's own website. Does it carry schema.org markup, clean hours, menu structure, location signals an agent can read?

VISIBILITY GAP — score 0-10. 0 = reputation and visibility aligned. 10 = high reputation, low/wrong visibility.

RELATIONSHIPS:
- competitors — 2-4 direct competitors in same city or cuisine bracket
- corridor_role — anchor / supporting / outlier. Major corridors: El Paseo (Palm Desert), Old Town La Quinta, downtown Palm Springs, The River (Rancho Mirage), festival-adjacent Indio, north Palm Desert development zone, uptown design district Palm Springs.
- query_wins — which queries it actually dominates

CONVERTIBILITY (the layer above the map):
- tier — "anchor-reference" (chain/franchise/resort-owned, no reachable local DM), "convertible" (independently owned, reachable, evidence of marketing spend), or "unclear"
- ownership_signal — independent / chain / franchise / resort-owned / hotel-restaurant / unclear
- marketing_spend_signal — what evidence of marketing budget? real site / paid listings / agency footprint / active social
- reasoning — one paragraph

sources_consulted: list URLs you actually consulted.
flags: anything you couldn't verify — list the unverified claim with [UNVERIFIED] tag.

Stay disciplined: do NOT invent awards, credentials, history, or specific stats. If you can't verify, flag it. The downstream verification layer will catch hallucinations.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- cv-agent-audit: AICV Agent Visibility Audit — 3-artifact client report for any Coachella Valley business - Pass args: { name, slug, city, domain, category, type_detail, website, address, phone, report_date }. Produces owner report, developer brief, and AICV node YAML seed.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:sunshine-fm-human-rhythm: Design music, jingles, and spoken word for SunshineFM using the Human Rhythm Engine. Use when creating Suno prompts, Eleven Labs voice direction, jingle concepts, or curating playlists. Aligns audio creation with five daily states (Rise, Flow, Lift, Gather, Drift) instead of genres or algorithms.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- cowork-plugin-management:cowork-plugin-customizer: Customize a Claude Code plugin for a specific organization's tools and workflows. Use when: customize plugin, set up plugin, configure plugin, tailor plugin, adjust plugin settings, customize plugin connectors, customize plugin skill, tweak plugin, modify plugin configuration.
- cowork-plugin-management:create-cowork-plugin: Guide users through creating a new plugin from scratch in a cowork session. Use when users want to create a plugin, build a plugin, make a new plugin, develop a plugin, scaffold a plugin, start a plugin from scratch, or design a plugin. This skill requires Cowork mode with access to the outputs directory for delivering the final .plugin file.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Cuistot in Palm Desert and Bernard Dervieux:

## Restaurant Overview

Cuistot is an elegant restaurant in Palm Desert, known for its French cuisine and carefully selected wine list, owned by Chef Bernard Dervieux, offering a country-modern ambiance with fireplaces and various dining spaces including quaint rooms, a patio, a bar, and a separate area for tasting menus.

## About Chef Bernard Dervieux

In 1987, he and his wife, Susan, opened Cuistot, and in 2003 built a new rustic French farmhouse in Palm Desert at the intersection of two of the most traveled thoroughfares in the California desert, and moved Cuistot to their new location. In 1974, Paul Bocuse sent Chef Bernard to work under Great Chef Jean Banchet as a saucier/sous chef at Le Français in Wheeling, Illinois, and in 1978 he became the executive chef at the Pump Room in Chicago before Great Chef Wolfgang Puck recommended him as executive chef at the Beverly Hills Hotel from 1980-1986.

## Reviews

Reviews are mixed. Positive reviews highlight: All of the food is delicious, traditional French cuisine with some of the best mushroom soup, beautiful interior, excellent service, and fantastic cocktails. Cuistot and Chef Bernard have gotten better at providing the Coachella Valley's superior restaurant, with food, ambience and service second to none.

However, some reviewers noted service issues. The service could use a speed boost, with waiting for drinks and food taking considerable time.

**Note:** Multiple sources indicate the restaurant may have recently closed, though some information appears current through 2025-2026.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Bernard Dervieux - Born 1953 in Condrieu, France. Trained under renowned chefs including Paul Bocuse and Roger Verge. Worked as Executive Chef at Beverly Hills Hotel (1980-1986) before opening Cuistot in 1987. Relocated to current location in 2003.

**Menu Structure:**

Award of Excellence designation mentioned; Award-winning wine list with moderate prices

**Special Features:**

- Private Wine Room (40 capacity)

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Cuistot in Palm Desert and its awards:

## Cuistot Awards and Recognition

Wine Spectator's 2013 Restaurant Wine List recognized Cuistot in Palm Desert with a Best of Award Excellence. This award gives special recognition to restaurants that clearly exceed the requirements of the Award of Excellence. Only 850 winners were selected across the globe.

Additionally, the restaurant is the first in the Coachella Valley to achieve a "Best of Award of Excellence" distinction from Wine Spectator.

## About the Restaurant

Cuistot is known for its flawless food, service and ambiance, with eclectic cuisine featuring French and Californian influences. Chef-owner Bernard Dervieux runs this charming restaurant, which offers quaint dining rooms, an outdoor patio, a bar, and a separate room for diners indulging in tasting menus.

The restaurant features an award-winning wine list. The eclectic cuisine includes dishes such as lobster ravioli, roasted rack of lamb, and sweetbread-stuffed quail.

Based on the search results, here are some of the best French restaurants in Palm Desert and the Coachella Valley for fine dining:

## Top Fine Dining Options

**Chez Pierre Bistro**
Honored as one of Palm Desert's Best French Restaurants, Chez Pierre Bistro has been recognized by Zagat, OpenTable Diners' Choice, Best Chefs of America, and Palm Springs Life Magazine. Located just a block from El Paseo, Chef Pierre Pelech transforms classic recipes into contemporary masterpieces, offering a true taste of France in the heart of the desert.

**Le Vallauris**
For over 30 years, Le Vallauris has been one of Palm Springs' most iconic fine dining destinations, with an elegant French-Mediterranean menu paired with impeccable service. Guests rave about the classic dishes from Dover sole to filet mignon, and the romantic garden patio, which is one of the most atmospheric dining experiences in the valley.

**French Rotisserie Café**
Nestled in Palm Desert, the French Rotisserie Cafe offers Contemporary European cuisine with an innovative "deconstructed" menu, where diners can craft their own plates with exquisite dishes like escargots, chicken vol-au-vent, and Boeuf Bourguignon.

**Café des Beaux-Arts**
Café des Beaux-Arts brings Parisian charm to El Paseo with French classics, fine wine, and outdoor dining. The joie de vivre and authentic Parisian cuisine has drummed up a loyal following over three decades of service, celebrating its 33rd anniversary in 2025.

**FARM**
FARM offers a nightly prix fixe dinner with multi-course French country cuisine that shifts with the seasons, featuring herbed fish, roasted vegetables, delicate soups, and rustic desserts.

Based on the search results, here are some of the best fine dining restaurants at El Paseo in Palm Desert for 2025:

## Prominent Fine Dining Options:

**Eddie V's** - This El Paseo restaurant features a posh happy hour, live jazz music, and enhancements to seafood and steaks including a cave-aged blue cheese crust. The establishment offers white tablecloths, crystal chandeliers and a dapper waitstaff, with overflowing seafood towers and perfectly plump oysters.

**Mastro's Steakhouse** - Mastro's Steakhouse Palm Desert provides a sophisticated dining experience and was recognized as an OpenTable Diners' Choice winner for 2025.

**Shorebird** - Luxury and leisure combine at this restaurant at The Shops on El Paseo, characterized by marble and gold accents, a sleek, modern interior and a decadent brunch menu. It blends modern coastal cuisine with timeless steakhouse classics, offering fresh seafood, premium steaks, and handcrafted cocktails in an elegant setting.

**Pacifica Seafood Restaurant** - Pacifica Seafood Restaurant serves the freshest seafood in the Desert, with seafood arriving daily from San Diego.

**La Spiga** - A fine dining establishment noted for celebrating fine dining experiences with a gorgeous patio and a team that elevates your dining experience.

A diverse selection of upscale and casual dining options ensures there's something for every taste on El Paseo.

content.text: I have enough to assess. Note the restaurant's own website still appears active, but Yelp/Gayot/Tripadvisor all confirm CLOSED with the property sold and being converted to El Paseo Chophouse. Now finalizing the structured output.

input.name: Cuistot

reputation.review_volume: 358 Tripadvisor reviews, Yelp shows 358 reviews / 226 photos; Google count not directly verified
reputation.sentiment: mostly positive historically (4.1/5 Tripadvisor) with mixed pockets in 2021-2023 about pricing/value; recent 2025 reviews returned to positive before closure
reputation.longevity: Operated ~38 years (1987-2025) under continuous Dervieux family ownership; original location 1987, moved to current El Paseo/Hwy 111 farmhouse in 2003; permanently closed in 2025, property sold (asking $6.1M; reported sale ~$5.1M) and being converted to 'El Paseo Chophouse'
credentials: Wine Spectator 'Best of Award of Excellence' (reported as first in Coachella Valley to receive it; 2013)
Chef Bernard Dervieux trained under Paul Bocuse, Roger Vergé; former Executive Chef Beverly Hills Hotel 1980-1986
Featured in Palm Springs Life, Coachella Valley Weekly, GAYOT, 10Best/USA Today
reputation.recency_signal: Reviews through spring 2025 (March/April) praised French cuisine, service (named servers Fabiola, Julian) and early bird pricing; closure announcement followed in 2025; multiple platforms (Yelp, Gayot, Tripadvisor) now flag CLOSED

agent_visibility.appears_in_queries: Surfaces strongly for 'best French restaurant Palm Desert', 'fine dining El Paseo', 'French fine dining Coachella Valley' across Tripadvisor, OpenTable, Yelp, Palm Springs Life, Wanderlog — but listings now carry stale 'open' signals on the restaurant's own site while aggregators show CLOSED. Does NOT consistently appear for 'date night Rancho Mirage' or breakfast queries (correctly out of scope).
agent_visibility.description_accuracy: Descriptions of cuisine, chef pedigree, and history are accurate across sources. Critical staleness: the restaurant's own domain (cuistotrestaurant.bar) still advertises current hours, prix fixe spring menu, and reservations as if operating — directly contradicting aggregator closure flags. Agents pulling from the official site would mislead users.
agent_visibility.site_structured_data: Official site (cuistotrestaurant.bar) is a marketing site with NAP and hours readable as text but NO detected schema.org Restaurant/LocalBusiness markup, no machine-readable menu structure, no openingHoursSpecification, no closure/permanently_closed signal. Aggregators (Yelp, Tripadvisor, Gayot) carry the closure status that the canonical site is missing.

competitors: Café des Beaux-Arts (El Paseo, Palm Desert)
relationships.corridor_role: Former anchor on El Paseo / Hwy 111 corner — one of the corridor's signature destination fine-dining rooms for nearly four decades; now a vacated anchor being repositioned as El Paseo Chophouse
query_wins: 'best French restaurant Palm Desert' (historically dominant alongside Chez Pierre)
'Bernard Dervieux' / chef-led French in the desert
'wine list Palm Desert fine dining' (650+ bottle list, Wine Spectator Best of Award of Excellence)

convertibility.marketing_spend_signal: Active branded website on non-standard TLD (.bar) with custom photography and menu pages; presence on OpenTable, Tripadvisor, Yelp, Vacation Palm Springs, Discover Palm Desert; legacy press in Palm Springs Life and Coachella Valley Weekly. No evidence of agency footprint or paid ad campaigns visible. However, the entity is no longer an operating business — it has been sold and is being converted to El Paseo Chophouse.
convertibility.reasoning: Cuistot was a classic independently-owned chef-driven restaurant (Bernard and Susan Dervieux) with clear marketing investment in its own site, OpenTable, and earned press — exactly the profile of a 'convertible' independent. BUT the business has effectively closed in 2025 and the property/operation transferred to new ownership as a chophouse concept. As 'Cuistot' the entity, it is no longer convertible — there is no going concern to onboard. The successor (El Paseo Chophouse) would need separate assessment. Marking 'unclear' because the legal entity may persist briefly and the Dervieux family may have future ventures, but for the purposes of this artifact Cuistot should be treated as a historical anchor-reference, not an active conversion target.

flags: [UNVERIFIED] Google review count band — Yelp 403'd on direct fetch; Tripadvisor shows 358 reviews at 4.1 stars but Google review total not independently confirmed
[UNVERIFIED] Exact closure date — multiple sources confirm 2025 closure and sale but precise final-service date not located in consulted sources
[UNVERIFIED] Final sale price — search summary cited $5.1M sale vs. $6.1M asking on Garland Commercial listing; sale-price confirmation not in directly fetched primary source
[UNVERIFIED] Wine Spectator 'first in Coachella Valley' claim — appears in Palm Springs Life headline summary but not independently corroborated against Wine Spectator records
[UNVERIFIED] 650+ wine list count cited in discovery brief — site mentions an 'award-winning wine list' but specific bottle count not verified on the official site content fetched

[STALE-CONTENT] Official cuistotrestaurant.bar site still presents the restaurant as operating with current hours and a 'Spring' prix fixe — contradicts Yelp/Gayot/Tripadvisor CLOSED status and the CoStar sale report
